trending_topics.context: 2 posts, 1.51x baseline rate вАФ mentioning Alex Karp, Palantir Technologies

sample_posts.text:
How will AI Labs Eventually Make Money
> The labs that survive wonвАЩt be the ones that make the API profitable.
> TheyвАЩll be the ones that identify their property above the station and build toward it now.
> The API is the rail. It will never be profitable enough.
> The money is in what appreciates around it.
Source:
https://michaellwy.substack.com/p/a-subway-company-solved-ais-business

Alex Karp on Enterprise AI вАФ
CNBC Interview
Filtered some substantive points worth chewing on:
1. The token pricing tell
If a model truly generates $1B in value, why charge per token instead of taking equity? Per-token pricing signals the vendor doesn't believe in the value they claim.
2. Margin concentrates in application layer and compute
(per Palantir's own financials)
The model layer runs thin to negative. Profit and free cash flow sit in the application layer and compute. Weak frontier lab financials come partly from the gap between token spend and actual value delivered.
3. Four question

After reflection, this new narrative by Palantir is probably much more consequential than people may assume.

Palantir is basically being the canary in the coal mine announcing the death of two major assumptions propping up the US economy right now: 

1) that AI labs will be able to extract significant economic rent - as opposed to AI models being mere commodities

2) that other countries can accept structural dependency on US technology and services without pushing back on sovereignty concerns

Why are Palantir specifically starting to be vocal about this?

sample_posts.text: https://x.com/mignano/status/2073080670365032808

tweet.text:
IвАЩm running out of reasons to believe the labs will have moats when itвАЩs all set and done.

Models? Unless someone reaches runaway self improvement, doesnвАЩt seem like it. Everyone catches up within months.

Compute? ThatвАЩs a cost, not a moat. Same chips for everyone.

Capital? You donвАЩt even need a working model to raise right now. Everyone wants in on AI. 

Talent? Follows the capital.

Training data? The webвАЩs been scraped to death. EveryoneвАЩs got the same stuff.

Lock-in? Routing makes models swappable. ItвАЩs a config change, maybe. And smart routing eliminates even that. 

Regulation? Good

sample_posts.text: https://x.com/lzminsky/status/2073033302064132119

tweet.text:
By no means am I an expert in compute markets, but was inspired to develop a take by both Brett and @0xfishylosopher 

Compute keeps getting scored against the commodity formation checklist and keeps failing the same two boxes, standardized delivery and time plus location pricing. Fair enough as history. 

Grain, oil and electricity all needed those things because their markets terminated in a dated futures contract, and a dated contract has to bridge into the physical world somehow. 

But new markets stopped terminating there. Perps collapsed the term structure into a funding rate and swapped

Compute? ThatвАЩs a cost, not a moat. Same chips for everyone.

Capital? You donвАЩt even need a working model to raise right now. Everyone wants in on AI. 

Talent? Follows the capital.

Training data? The webвАЩs been scraped to death. EveryoneвАЩs got the same stuff.

Lock-in? Routing makes models swappable. ItвАЩs a config change, maybe. And smart routing eliminates even that. 

Regulation? Good

Palantir is basically being the canary in the coal mine announcing the death of two major assumptions propping up the US economy right now:

2) that other countries can accept structural dependency on US technology and services without pushing back on sovereignty concerns
